current_fact.fact_text: In 1934, Australia’s Eastern Mission, led by politician John Latham, departed on a three‑month diplomatic tour of East and Southeast Asia. It marked one of Australia’s early attempts to shape its own regional foreign policy rather than relying solely on British ties.

episode.title: Morning Drops #32 - A Weekend In London
episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town. Four tracks to ease you into the week at the right pace.
songs: Jimmy Castor Bunch - It's Just Begun
The Asphodells - Late Flowering Lust (Phil Kieran Remix)

current_fact.match_reason: A diplomatic tour rethinking regional relationships fits with an episode about experiencing a global capital through movement, meetings, and cross‑cultural sounds.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1925, Tennessee passed the Butler Act, banning the teaching of human evolution in public schools. This law set the stage for the famous Scopes “Monkey” Trial and a fierce public debate over science, religion, and who gets to define truth in the classroom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A law that tried to freeze scientific thought contrasts with an episode centered on unlearning, rethinking, and allowing beliefs to evolve.

queue.fact_text: That same year, 1925, saw the premiere of Maurice Ravel’s whimsical opera "L'enfant et les sortilèges" in Monte Carlo, with a libretto by the writer Colette. The story turns a child’s tantrum into a surreal night where household objects and animals come alive to scold and transform him.

episode.title: Sedna FM #04 - Queen of Spades
episode.description: Tuning in from the planet of irrational love. Exploring love's most vulnerable side — the kind that awakens the child within us. A diverse music selection ranging from electric blues to downtempo, soul, and jazz. A tribute to the feeling that has moved artists to create the most beautiful works. Love makes us vulnerable, and there is no art without vulnerability.
songs: Alabama Shakes - Gimme All Your Love
Queens of the stone age - Make it wit chu
Etta James - I'd Rather Go Blind
Eric Clapton - Have you ever loved a woman

queue.match_reason: A magical opera about a child confronted by animated objects pairs beautifully with an episode about irrational love and childlike vulnerability in art.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1844, the Baháʼí calendar began its first year, later celebrated annually as Naw‑Rúz, the Baháʼí New Year. The calendar is built from 19‑day months and emphasizes spiritual renewal alongside the turning of earthly seasons.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

queue.match_reason: A spiritual New Year tied to the rhythm of nature aligns with an episode evoking cyclical time, warmth, and quiet renewal through an ‘endless summer’ mood.

queue.fact_text: In 1928, Charles Lindbergh received the Medal of Honor for his solo nonstop flight across the Atlantic the previous year. His 1927 journey in the Spirit of St. Louis turned a 33‑hour gamble into a symbol of aviation’s future and the shrinking of oceans.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A nerve‑wracking solo flight over the Atlantic suits the episode’s high‑voltage rock and blues, echoing the adrenaline and experimentation of early aviation.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1990, Namibia gained independence after 75 years under South African administration, much of it during the apartheid era. The new nation emerged after a long liberation struggle, becoming one of Africa’s youngest countries.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: Namibia’s independence and the Tuareg’s electrified desert blues both speak to African landscapes, resistance, and the sound of people reclaiming their land and story.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1970, what would become San Diego Comic‑Con opened its doors for the first time as a small comics convention. Its humble launch planted the seed for a massive global gathering that now shapes how entire generations experience stories and heroes.

episode.title: Morning Drops #30 - Star Treatment
episode.description: A celebration of change and transformation. Gaia traces the evolution of Arctic Monkeys - from raw energy to cinematic sound - reflecting on how artists, and all of us, grow and change through time. A journey through courage, curiosity, and the beauty of slowing down.
songs: Arctic Monkeys - There’d Better Be a Mirrorball

queue.match_reason: Comic‑Con’s rise from niche gathering to star‑studded spectacle echoes the evolution of a band from scrappy beginnings to full ‘star treatment’ and cinematic staging.

queue.fact_text: On March 21, 1980, the Moondog Coronation Ball’s spiritual descendants were everywhere, but another quiet milestone in music tech was about to reshape sound: within that era, tools like pitch correction and later Auto‑Tune would emerge from labs to redefine how voices are heard. The seeds of today’s hyper‑processed pop were just being planted.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: This fact zooms out on a turning‑point era in music technology, directly matching an episode dedicated to unpacking how Auto‑Tune quietly took over modern sound.

queue.fact_text: Across history, March 21 has also often marked the spring equinox in the Northern Hemisphere — the moment when day and night are nearly equal and many cultures celebrate balance and the return of light. It’s a natural reset button built into the planet’s orbit.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

queue.match_reason: The equinox’s quiet balance and planetary rhythm harmonize with an episode called ‘Slow Planet’, inviting listeners to breathe with Earth’s own tempo.

queue.fact_text: For many Baháʼís, March 21’s Naw‑Rúz is celebrated not just as a calendar reset but as the end of a 19‑day fast — a day of feasting, music, and gathering after intentional restraint. It blends cosmic timing, personal discipline, and communal joy in a single date.

episode.title: Morning Drops #09 - Birthday Cake
episode.description: Today's breakfast comes with a sprinkle of stardust and a slice of birthday cake as we celebrate a pure and unique soul, Yasmin. On Sedna, we may not do years, but we sure know how to party!

queue.match_reason: A spiritual New Year’s feast after a long fast fits perfectly with a celebratory, cake‑filled episode — both mark endings, beginnings, and the joy of gathering.

published.fact_text: In 1965, NASA launched Ranger 9, the final probe in a daring series of missions that deliberately crashed into the Moon. Before impact, Ranger 9 transmitted high‑resolution close‑up images of the lunar surface, helping scientists choose safer landing sites for future crewed Apollo missions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A Moon‑crashing probe turning empty space into data echoes the episode’s theme of transforming ‘emptiness’ into creative potential and forward motion.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1952, DJ Alan Freed hosted the Moondog Coronation Ball in Cleveland, often hailed as the first major rock and roll concert. The event was so wildly oversold that thousands of fans were left outside and the show was shut down after just one song, revealing how explosive this new sound had become.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

published.match_reason: The first explosive rock concert parallels Sedna’s own origin episode — both are chaotic, foundational moments that ignite a new musical journey.

published.fact_text: In 1871, journalist Henry Morton Stanley set off on a perilous expedition through central Africa to find the long‑missing explorer and missionary David Livingstone. His eventual greeting, “Dr. Livingstone, I presume?”, became one of history’s most famous lines, symbolizing both Victorian exploration and its myths.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A months‑long trek to find a man in the interior mirrors a modern shared voyage by boat — both are about endurance, navigation, and the stories we collect along a journey.

published.fact_text: During the Napoleonic Wars, the 1814 Battle of Arcis‑sur‑Aube saw Austrian forces turn back Napoleon’s army in eastern France. It was one of his last major battles on home soil, a moment when his aura of invincibility began to fracture.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A decisive clash that undermines a powerful empire fits the militant, anti‑authoritarian energy of a Rage Against the Machine‑focused episode.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1999, Bertrand Piccard and Brian Jones completed the first nonstop balloon flight around the Earth. After 19 days aloft in the Breitling Orbiter 3, they landed in Egypt, proving that a fragile‑looking balloon could quietly do what airplanes had raced to achieve in the 20th century.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: A serene circumnavigation of Earth by balloon pairs with an episode about travel as a calm, perspective‑shifting meditation rather than a rush.

published.fact_text: In 1994, the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change came into force, creating a global treaty framework to confront human‑driven climate change. It was a quiet legal step that would later underpin landmark agreements like the Kyoto Protocol and the Paris Agreement.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

published.match_reason: A global climate treaty and Afrobeat share roots in political awakening and calls for environmental and social justice across the Global South.

published.fact_text: In 1985, Canadian athlete Rick Hansen began his 'Man in Motion' World Tour, wheeling his wheelchair through 34 countries to raise awareness and funds for spinal cord injury research. Over 26 months, he covered more than 40,000 km, turning personal adversity into a global movement.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Hansen’s grueling, incremental world tour reflects the episode’s focus on persistence, slow progress, and finding meaning through effortful challenge.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1804, France adopted the Napoleonic Code, a civil law system that emphasized clear, written rules and equality before the law for (male) citizens. Its structure proved so influential that it helped shape legal systems from Europe to Latin America and beyond.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: A code that rewired how society is organized parallels an episode about the unwritten ‘rules’ our devices now impose, and how we might consciously rewrite them.

published.fact_text: In 1980, U.S. President Jimmy Carter announced that the United States would boycott the Moscow Olympics to protest the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan. More than 60 countries eventually joined, turning a global celebration of sport into a stage for Cold War geopolitics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: An Olympic boycott highlights the political weight behind sport, while the Thailand episode focuses on martial arts and movement, connecting body, discipline, and global politics of sport.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1970, San Francisco’s mayor Joseph Alioto issued the first municipal Earth Day proclamation, helping to launch what would become a worldwide environmental observance. The idea was radical at the time: dedicate a civic holiday to the health of the planet itself.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: The birth of Earth Day pairs with intimate stories of orcas and Arctic seas, both centering the planet’s fragile ecosystems and why they deserve their own kind of holiday.

published.fact_text: Also in 1970, San Diego Comic‑Con held its first event, a modest one‑day gathering that would grow into one of the world’s largest pop culture conventions. What began as a meeting for comic fans evolved into a global nexus for film, TV, games, and fandom.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - secret_mp3_player_1
episode.description: Inspired by a throwback MP3 player on Instagram, Yasmin and Gaia take you on a nostalgic journey to the early 2000s. LimeWire, eMule, guilty-pleasure playlists — remember those? Hit play and relive the songs that shaped a generation.

published.match_reason: Comic‑Con’s evolution into a massive pop‑culture festival resonates with an episode steeped in youthful nostalgia, fandom, and the shared media obsessions of a generation.

published.fact_text: On March 21, 1965, Martin Luther King Jr. led 3,200 marchers from Selma on the third, finally successful trek to Montgomery, Alabama. The marchers walked 54 miles under federal protection, and their courage helped pave the way for the U.S. Voting Rights Act of 1965.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

published.match_reason: A historic march for civil rights aligns with an episode built around conscious hip hop and unboxing identities, continuing the struggle for racial justice through music and reflection.

published.fact_text: In 1945, British forces captured Mandalay in Burma (now Myanmar), a key objective in the Allied campaign to push Japanese troops out of Southeast Asia. The battle transformed the city’s hilltop fort into a symbol of liberation after years of occupation.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: Mandalay’s liberation near the end of a brutal war ties into an episode about storms ending and new chapters beginning, framed in reflective, hopeful rock.

published.fact_text: That same day in 1945, the RAF launched Operation Carthage, a low‑level air raid on the Gestapo headquarters in Copenhagen. While the main target was badly damaged, a tragic navigational error led bombers to hit a school, killing 125 civilians and underscoring the terrible risks of precision bombing.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: An operation that mixed targeted resistance with devastating collateral damage mirrors Residente’s politically charged storytelling about power, violence, and unintended consequences.

published.fact_text: In 1934, Australia’s Eastern Mission, led by politician John Latham, departed on a three‑month diplomatic tour of East and Southeast Asia. It marked one of Australia’s early attempts to shape its own regional foreign policy rather than relying solely on British ties.

episode.description: Recorded in London, marking a weekend reunion between Gaia and Yasmin. A selection inspired by London's energy, seen through the eyes of two inseparable friends — music from concerts, club nights, and long walks across town.
